raw_brief:
PURPOSE
pixiedining.com is a paid subscription service that sends instant alerts when hard-to-book Disney World restaurants get cancellations. A visitor lands on the page and should be able to decide, within seconds, whether to pay $6.99/month for unlimited alerts to their phone (push notification), SMS, and email. The primary success event is the visitor clicking the $6.99/month CTA in the hero and completing payment. A secondary path is the pricing page, which is where the $49/year plan is presented as an upsell.

AUDIENCE
The first paying customer is an individual Disney trip planner — a foodie or family vacation planner who has been personally trying and failing to land a reservation at a hard-to-book Disney restaurant. The founder picked individuals over agency concierges when forced to choose, on the gut judgment that agencies may not actually use these tools. The canonical pain story comes from the founder's own experience: more than 50 manual checks across days for Beak and Barrel, all without landing the reservation, fitting them in around lunch, work hours, and bathroom breaks. Disney's 60-day booking window plus its no-show charge means committed slots get cancelled close to the date, which is the supply of openings the alerts are catching.

CONTEXT
Visitors arrive cold and skeptical. The founder's primary acquisition motion is dropping helpful replies in Reddit threads (e.g., r/WaltDisneyWorld) and Disney-planning Facebook groups when someone vents about not being able to book a specific restaurant — comments first, content (Instagram, TikTok) and Google SEO later. Competitors are named and framed: DreamTable was tried personally and burned the founder with 10-15 minute alert lag and broken push notifications; Mouse Dining charges per alert. pixiedining.com's wedge is instant alerts plus a flat, cheap subscription — explicitly positioned as cheaper than a coffee for a Western audience. The aesthetic is playful and magical, in the spirit of Disney parks blogs the audience already reads, rather than a sterile utility/SaaS look.

KEY ACTIONS
The hero must do two things in the first viewport: (1) show a short phone-screen recording of an alert firing on a lock screen seconds after a cancellation, as the trust anchor — the founder explicitly chose this over a live public ticker when forced to pick one proof element; (2) present the $6.99/month CTA directly, with no free trial and no free first alert by deliberate choice. Trust is carried by the proof video and by the page simply not emitting untrustworthy signals — there is no money-back guarantee, no live ticker, no testimonials specified. The pricing page is where the $49/year plan does the upselling work; it should not compete for attention in the hero. Beak and Barrel should appear by name as the concrete restaurant that grounds the pain, since that is the specific hook the founder is willing to put on the page.
optional_business_context: B2C self-serve subscription. $6.99/month for unlimited push, SMS, and email alerts on Disney World restaurant cancellations; $49/year plan exists as an upsell on the pricing page, not in the hero. No free trial, no free first alert. Primary monetization is monthly subscription with annual upgrade path post-click.
optional_known_constraints: Must not emit any untrustworthy signals — visitors are skeptical and several have been burned by buggy competitors. No free trial or free first alert. Hero must lead with the $6.99/month CTA, not the annual plan. The phone-screen recording of an alert firing on a lock screen is the required above-the-fold proof element.
optional_user_preferences: Playful and magical aesthetic in the spirit of Disney parks blogs the audience already reads (warm, whimsical), explicitly not a stripped-down utility/SaaS look. Direct, low-friction path from CTA to payment.
optional_existing_site_or_brand_notes: New site at pixiedining.com, nothing existing to preserve. Competitors named as foils, not partners: DreamTable (buggy, 10-15 minute alert lag, broken push) and Mouse Dining (per-alert pricing). Beak and Barrel is the canonical example restaurant to name on the page.
